title: Building a hay rack for livestock
subtitle: Positioning and filling a wall-mounted or free-standing hay rack for sheep, goats, or housed cattle over the winter.
excerpt: A hay rack keeps forage off the ground, reduces waste from trampling, and allows multiple animals to eat simultaneously without competing at a pile on the floor. The rack height and bar spacing vary by species: what suits a sheep will trap the horns of a goat, and a rack designed for adult cattle is too high for a weaned calf. Getting these details right before the rack is fixed avoids having to move it later.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Cattle (2003). https://www.gov.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: before you decide where to put it. A rack that cannot fit every animal at once means some get pushed off at every feed. With ad-lib hay this matters less. With set rations it matters a lot. The shy animals in the group are usually the ones that can least afford to miss a meal.

Set the bottom bar at shoulder height for the species. That means about 60 to 70 cm off the floor for adult sheep, 70 to 80 cm for adult goats, and 80 to 100 cm for beef cattle. At this height the animal reaches in to the rack on the level. It does not crane up (which strains the neck and tips chaff into the eyes) or pull hay down at a steep angle, which wastes it.

For polled (hornless) sheep, a standard rack with bars spaced 10 to 12 cm apart allows the head to enter and withdraw without trapping. For horned breeds or goats, wider spacing of 15 to 18 cm is required, or use a keyhole-shaped opening that the horns cannot enter at all. A goat that traps its horns in a hay rack and cannot withdraw will damage its neck or suffocate if it panics and falls. Check bar spacing before any horned animal is left unattended with a new rack.
Position the rack against a solid wall with a concrete or compacted base beneath it rather than over a bedded area.

that falls onto clean concrete can be swept and re-fed; hay that falls into deep straw bedding is lost. A simple steel lip or collection tray below the rack catches loose material. Fill the rack to two-thirds capacity rather than packing it full: overfilling increases the rate at which hay is pulled out and dropped.
